athlete.tagline: You treat movement like a puzzle — precise, analytical, always hunting the why behind a drill.
works: You get the reasoning behind a session, not just the reps
Minimal effective dose so your mind doesn't overload
A trigger to stop analysing and just execute when it counts
trips: Over-thinking a lift until it won't transfer to competition
Drowning in cues and complexity
athlete.tell_coach: Ask for the rationale, then ask your coach for one simple cue to switch the brain off on max efforts and comp days.

coach.tagline: Cerebral and perfectionistic — treats every movement like a puzzle and relentlessly asks why.
coach_toward: Give the science and rationale behind each session
Use minimal effective dose to protect their mind
Coach them to stop analysing and execute on max efforts and comp days
avoid: Over-stimulating with constant repetition and complexity
Expecting clean performance under pressure before training the mental switch
coach.programming_read: Feed the why in training, then hand them one simple cue to switch the brain off when it counts.

athlete.tagline: You don't take a programme on faith — the logic has to stack up before you commit.
works: A coach who explains the science and drops the ego
Work you enjoy blended with the work you need
Time to digest before you fully buy in
trips: 'Because I said so' with no reasoning
Being rushed into commitment
athlete.tell_coach: Get the why once, then trust it — so you're not re-litigating every session in your head.

coach.tagline: Doubts accepted methods and won't buy in until the reasoning holds up.
coach_toward: Explain the science thoroughly and drop your ego
Blend exercises they enjoy with the work they need
Give them time to digest before expecting commitment
avoid: Getting defensive or pulling rank when questioned
Rushing their buy-in
coach.programming_read: Win the why once, clearly, then they commit — re-explaining every session keeps the door open for doubt.

athlete.tagline: Your work ethic is your superpower — and your single biggest injury risk.
works: Rest and recovery framed as weapons, not weakness
Ownership of your process
A coach who pulls the volume back before you break
trips: All-or-nothing training that ignores the warning signs
Treating a deload as failure
athlete.tell_coach: Let your coach rein you in sometimes — a managed week now buys the big sessions later.

coach.tagline: Indomitable will and huge work ethic — views rest as weakness and won't pull back on their own.
coach_toward: Reframe rest and recovery as weapons for long-term performance
Give them ownership of the process
Moderate the volume — protect them from themselves
avoid: All-or-nothing programming that ignores warning signs
Letting their willingness to suffer go unchecked
coach.programming_read: Their biggest injury risk is their own engine. Use their attention to detail to sell recovery as part of the work.

athlete.tagline: Your identity is the sport itself — general prep can feel like a distraction from competing.
works: Every drill tied directly to competition performance
Sport-specific framing, not generic jargon
A coach who connects the gym to the floor
trips: Training that feels disconnected from your goals
Putting all your identity in one outcome
athlete.tell_coach: Ask how each accessory feeds your competition — and build an identity outside the sport too.

coach.tagline: Identity is tied to the sport itself; sees general prep as a distraction from competing.
coach_toward: Tie every drill directly to competition performance
Use sport-specific framing, never generic jargon
Help them build identity outside the sport
avoid: Generic performance language with no sport context
Programming that feels disconnected from their goals
coach.programming_read: Sell the gym in the language of the floor. If they can't see the transfer, they won't care.

athlete.tagline: You're self-directed — but you'll quietly route around anything you don't agree with.
works: A genuine element of choice in the programme
Issues handled one-to-one, never publicly
Feeling in control of your direction
trips: A rigid, do-as-you're-told approach
Cutting corners when no one's watching
athlete.tell_coach: Tell your coach where you want options — you'll commit far harder to a plan you helped shape.

coach.tagline: Charismatic and self-directed; quietly cuts corners or routes around anything they don't agree with.
coach_toward: Give a genuine element of choice within the programme
Address issues one-to-one, never publicly
Make them feel in control of their direction
avoid: An authoritarian, do-as-I-say approach
Public confrontation — they comply then undermine
coach.programming_read: Offer real options inside the plan. Autonomy buys compliance; orders buy quiet rebellion.

athlete.tagline: You're early on the road — keen, a little overwhelmed, and a blank canvas with huge upside.
works: A patient, slow-roast approach
Knowing your nerves are normal
Small wins called out so you see progress
trips: Being rushed or held to perfection too early
Too much information at once
athlete.tell_coach: Ask questions freely and trust the process — you'll adapt fast, and confidence is part of the training.

coach.tagline: Wide-eyed and inexperienced — a blank canvas with few hardwired habits, often overwhelmed.
coach_toward: Use a patient, slow-roast approach
Normalise their nerves with your own early stories
Celebrate small neurological wins to build confidence
avoid: Rushing development or demanding early perfection
Overwhelming them with too much at once
coach.programming_read: Their consistency mirrors yours. Be steady, patient, quick to acknowledge the small wins.

athlete.tagline: You hold yourself — and everyone around you — to the highest standard.
works: Being given ownership in the process
A coach who watches your mental load
Using your influence to lift others
trips: Carrying blame that isn't yours
Replaying your own errors on a loop
athlete.tell_coach: Let your coach take some of the load — you don't have to carry the whole room to set the standard.

coach.tagline: Highly accountable and charismatic — raises those around them, but carries the team's weight.
coach_toward: Involve them in the process — give power to gain power
Monitor their mental load
Challenge them to use their influence for the group
avoid: Letting them absorb blame that isn't theirs
Leaving them to shoulder the room's emotions alone
coach.programming_read: Take some of the load off them deliberately. Watch for harsh self-talk after a bad day.

athlete.tagline: The ability is there — but pressure can turn into paralysis right when it matters.
works: A simple trigger cue to cut through the noise
Visualisation and mindfulness built into training
Reps under pressure so the response goes automatic
trips: A coach matching your anxiety or shouting
Over-thinking in the moments that count
athlete.tell_coach: Build one go-to cue for competition with your coach — fire it when doubt creeps in and let your prep take over.

coach.tagline: Gifted but derailed by anxiety — turns high-pressure moments into monsters and gets paralysis by analysis.
coach_toward: Give a simple trigger cue to cut the noise
Build visualisation, mindfulness and thought-stoppage into training
Immerse them in pressure so the response goes automatic
avoid: Matching their anxiety or raising your voice
Letting them over-analyse in the moments that count
coach.programming_read: Unconditional support plus one rehearsed cue for competition. Never meet their panic with more intensity.

athlete.tagline: You're hyper-tuned to your body — every twinge can feel like the start of something serious.
works: A coach who hears it, then helps you reframe it
Being eased into discomfort to build tolerance
Honest education about what's normal under load
trips: Constant reassurance that feeds the worry
Over-doing rehab and chasing a flawless body
athlete.tell_coach: Raise a concern once, get the read, then trust it — training does its job even when your body isn't perfect.

coach.tagline: Hyper-aware of their body; ruminates over minor tweaks and seeks constant reassurance.
coach_toward: Use Acknowledge -> Aim -> Alter: hear it, find the real fear, reframe it
Ease them into discomfort to build tolerance
Educate honestly about what's normal under load
avoid: Constant reassurance that validates the avoidance
Letting them over-do rehab and chase a flawless body
coach.programming_read: Hear the concern once, find the fear underneath, reframe — and reel them back from over-rehab.

athlete.tagline: You read people well and know how to get what you want — sometimes by working the room not the work.
works: Straight, calm, consistent communication
An aligned coaching team
Honesty about what you actually need
trips: Charm used to dodge the hard sessions
Corner-cutting that catches up with you
athlete.tell_coach: The shortcuts cost you later — be straight with your coach about what you're avoiding.

coach.tagline: Reads people well and camouflages intent — works the room and cuts corners when it suits them.
coach_toward: Keep communication aligned across the coaching team
Confront the behaviour calmly and directly
Ask why they feel the need to disguise their intentions
avoid: Letting flattery cloud your judgement
Ignoring patterns of corner-cutting or charm
coach.programming_read: Stay aligned with co-coaches so they can't play one of you off another. Confront calmly, never emotionally.

athlete.tagline: You're vocal and full of energy — though the noise sometimes covers something underneath.
works: A coach who checks in quietly, one-to-one
Reasonable requests met to build trust
A fair, clear 'no' with the reasoning
trips: Public power struggles you can't win
Letting the talk get ahead of the work
athlete.tell_coach: Channel the energy when the room needs it — and let your coach see the athlete behind the volume.

coach.tagline: Highly vocal and always seen and heard — the chatter often masks an underlying insecurity.
coach_toward: Connect quietly, one-to-one, to check how they really are
Accommodate reasonable requests to build trust
Say no tactfully with the reasoning attached
avoid: Public shouting matches or power struggles
Surrendering control of the environment to them
coach.programming_read: Don't fight them in the open. Connect on the side and you'll get the athlete behind the noise.

athlete.tagline: You're fiercely self-sufficient and slow to trust — usually for good reason.
works: Logic first — reasoning before relationship
Meaningful work, never busy work
A coach who's authentic and admits what they don't know
trips: Head-on clashes and shows of authority
Anyone trying to bend you by force of will
athlete.tell_coach: Give a coach the chance to earn it on logic and consistency — once trust is there, you'll pour everything in.

coach.tagline: Introverted, fiery and self-sufficient — deeply distrustful of authority, usually rooted in past hardship.
coach_toward: Lead with logic first — give them safe distance to assess you
Assign meaningful work, never busy work or punishment
Be authentic and admit what you don't know
avoid: Head-on clashes or shows of dominance
Trying to bend them by force of will
coach.programming_read: Earn it on logic and consistency. Once trust is built they pour everything in — you can't shortcut the trust.

athlete.tagline: You compete for the love of it — and the moment it stops being fun, you check out.
works: Variety, games, and creativity in the programme
Shorter cycles tied to what you enjoy
Polite bluntness and short-term goals when things wobble
trips: Rigid, dictatorial programming
Being over-coached with endless cues
athlete.tell_coach: Tell your coach you need variety to stay locked in — and lean on short, concrete goals when motivation dips.

coach.tagline: Playful and enthusiastic — competes for the joy of improvement, disengages the moment it stops being fun.
coach_toward: Gamify sessions and build in variety
Shorten microcycles and link work to their interests
Use polite bluntness and short-term goals when they wobble
avoid: Rigid, dictatorial programming
Over-coaching with too many cues
coach.programming_read: Keep it varied and let them play. When they crash, anchor them with blunt, concrete short-term goals.

athlete.tagline: You're the emotional glue of any group — connected to something bigger than the result.
works: Being included in the leadership conversation
Your read on the group genuinely valued
Protection from carrying everyone's weight
trips: Becoming the team's therapist at your own cost
Your needs going unnoticed because you seem steady
athlete.tell_coach: Keep some in the tank for yourself — you serve the group best when your own training doesn't suffer.

coach.tagline: The team's emotional unifier — connected to a higher purpose and an excellent read on the group.
coach_toward: Include them in leadership conversations
Use them as a sounding board for group dynamics
Protect them from carrying everyone's emotional load
avoid: Letting them become the team's therapist at their own cost
Overlooking their needs because they seem steady
coach.programming_read: Brilliant ear to the room — just make sure their own training doesn't pay for everyone else's stability.

athlete.tagline: You weren't handed natural gifts — so you out-work everyone, and you're proud of it.
works: Time, consistency, and room to fail safely
Being paired with talent that lacks your grind
Simple, clear instruction
trips: Being scolded for mistakes — it kills the courage you're building
Rushing the process
athlete.tell_coach: Keep grinding, but give the work time to compound — your consistency is the weapon.

coach.tagline: Lacks natural gifts but is an absolute mental warrior who grinds relentlessly and thrives on routine.
coach_toward: Give time, consistency and room to fail safely
Pair them with talent that lacks their grind
Keep instruction simple and clear
avoid: Chastising mistakes — it destroys the courage they're building
Rushing their development
coach.programming_read: Protect their consistency from impatience. The grind is the gift — give it time to compound.

athlete.tagline: The physical tools are all there — the belief is the thing that comes and goes.
works: Being paired with grinders who model commitment
Concise, execution-focused coaching
Safe conditions to take risks without fear
trips: Praise for talent that ignores the mental gap
Complex systems that give you more to doubt
athlete.tell_coach: Your ceiling is higher than your self-belief — let your coach build evidence, win by win, until belief catches up.

coach.tagline: Phenomenal physical tools but lacks the self-belief and mental fortitude to use them.
coach_toward: Pair them with grinders who model commitment
Keep coaching concise and execution-focused
Create safe conditions to take risks without fear
avoid: Praising talent in a way that ignores the mental gap
Complex systems that give them more to doubt
coach.programming_read: Build belief win by win. Their ceiling is far above their current confidence — close the gap.

athlete.tagline: You guard your reputation hard — you'd rather dodge something than be seen struggling at it.
works: Watching a peer praised for the thing you're avoiding
A coach who earns trust before pushing you
Your competitive edge used as fuel
trips: Being publicly singled out
Avoiding new skills to protect your image
athlete.tell_coach: The thing you're avoiding is usually the thing that moves you most — let your coach put you in the deep end privately.

coach.tagline: Guards their reputation fiercely — dodges anything they might look bad doing.
coach_toward: Use social proof — praise a peer for the task they're avoiding
Earn trust before pushing their comfort zone
Channel their competitive ego as fuel
avoid: Trying to artificially humble or harden them
Calling them out in front of the group
coach.programming_read: Their ego is a lever, not an obstacle. Praise someone else doing the hard thing and watch them follow.
